title: Internal and External Descriptions of a System
summary: This page discusses the distinction between external and internal system descriptions using a balanced bridge circuit example with a capacitor. It demonstrates that for zero initial conditions, the capacitor is undetectable from external measurements, as the circuit behaves equivalently to a simpler resistive network with y(t) = (2/5)x(t). The page concludes that while external and internal descriptions are often equivalent, internal descriptions can reveal signals invisible to external observation.

figures.caption: A system that cannot be described by external measurements.
figures.description: The figure shows two equivalent circuit diagrams labeled (a) and (b) in a signals-and-systems context, with input voltage source x(t) on the left, output y(t) measured at right-side terminals marked + and -, and current i indicated through a 3 Ω series resistor. In (a), a balanced bridge network has two vertical branches of 2 Ω resistors with midpoint nodes a and b connected by a capacitor, and the branch currents are labeled i/2, showing equal current splitting. In (b), the bridge is replaced by an externally equivalent 1 Ω + 1 Ω vertical branch after the same 3 Ω resistor. Students should notice that the internal capacitor and node structure in (a) are not revealed by external terminal measurements alone, even though the two circuits appear equivalent from the outside.
